title: Threads for rtfeldman
author: Rtfeldman Edited; On Proposal Add As A Flag Reason
text:
This is a textbook example of what tags are for and flags are not for: "Lots of people are submitting and upvoting a category of content that others don't like."
We already have an off-topic flag. If a post is on-topic, not spam, etc., and getting upvoted a lot, then flagging it makes no sense. The upvotes indicate people want to see it. If others don't want to see it, that's exactly why we have tag + filter: so it's only seen by those who are interested. (If you want all LLM-related posts to be considered off-topic for Lobsters, that's a different proposal.)
We don't need a new flag, we need to replace the vibecoding tag. Neither people who want to see LLM-related content nor people who don't want to see LLM-related content seem to be happy with how that tag is working out.
It's not simply a matter of tagging though. There are posts being removed with the reason "slop." Those stories don't belong on the site. That's a flagging reason, not a tagging one.
This is persuasive. Put me in the Tag not Flag camp.
My issue is not with pro or anti AI posts, but when they lack technical depth. Show me what you learned about the problem domain, or your agent setup or the prompts or token costs or model differences. I've read so many incredible technical articles posted on lobste.rs and I would love to learn more about the "how" of agentic development, not just how it's going to take my job (or not).
Maybe this implies a new kind of flag: fluff
. If an article is lacking technical details, but it is otherwise on topic, tag it with fluff
to downrank it.
That sounds like a totally reasonable flag to have!
If it is lacking technical detail/depth, then it is also insufficiently high quality for this site and shouldn't be posted
We don't want to use tags for things that aren't high quality and shouldn't be here, we want to use tags to organize stuff that deserves to be here
But once something is mistakenly posted on the site, how do you indicate that it should be removed or at least down ranked? If you look at the current flags, you can choose from off topic, spam, already posted, or broken link. I think fluff would be a good supplement to those flags.
Oh! My mistake, I misread what you wrote. I thought you were proposing a new tag
, not a new flag
. Yes, a new flag
seems appropriate
No worries!
I agree. The blog post about redoing all their manual commits using an Agent as an exercise for getting better was interesting. It opened my mind at “agentic exercises”. It reminds me of when I wanted to learn prompt injection, but I really just wanted a game built around it to practice. Maybe I’ll do an Karpathy Koans thing (like Kotlin Koans)
Yes, and that's exactly the kind of posts that are on-topic and appreciated by the community. But it has become an unfortunate truth that the vast majority of the posts in "vibecoding" tag are not like that. A vast majority of the posts in this tag are low effort opinion pieces with no technical content. They are literally spam according to me. Maybe not by this site's definition of spam, but for me they're just spam. I don't know what it'd take to revert Lobsters to the state where we can get rid of these low effort opinion pieces and promote the good ones like the one you described.
An alternative tag called opinion
with a negative hotness factor?
Show me what you learned about the problem domain
LLMs have absorbed a collective misunderstanding of OOP. Not surprising since most developers and IDE tools implement accessor methods without batting an eye.
https://dave.autonoma.ca/blog/2026/02/03/lloopy-loops/
That said, there's definitely a balance to be made. After all, who wants to codify syntax highlighting rules for 40-ish programming languages, when an LLM can churn out the code with unparalleled speed and accuracy?
https://repo.autonoma.ca/repo/treetrek/blob/HEAD/render/LanguageDefinitions.php
I think roc-lang.org is already doing 100% of these (or at least, the new compiler is doing them - which is not quite ready for daily-driver use, but we're getting very close!)
Checklist from the article:
Maybe I missed something, but pretty cool to see someone write "A Language For Agents" in February 2026 which describes a language whose first commit was made in 2019! :D
turns out that good thing for humans are also good for agents, but since humans adapt easily we cope with "less" than ideal. Agents are just less adaptable and we might need to have better tools in the end thanks to it.
Lobsters is a polite garden party for very loud AI salesmen. Objecting to the garden party having been taken over by loud AI salesmen is impolite.
My impression is that the median commenter on Lobsters is vocally anti-LLM, and the use of the vibecoding tag fits--it's an indication that most vocal people would like to mock the use of LLMs.
Personally, I'm...either mildly skeptical, or mildly positive, depending on where you sit. I've used LLMs at work, and will keep experimenting with them, and suspect that they will continue to have some real role in my workflow even if they don't improve. However, I also find them to be patchy in their capabilities, and have never shipped a feature that was majority written by LLMs without significant human effort to clean up the results. Probably a majority of the things I've worked on over the last three months have featured zero use of LLMs to write code.
It's great when the LLM works, but the hit or miss aspect is tiring, and I wonder what's different for the people who claim much better results. But from that position, I find myself significantly less negative than the median comment on any LLM related topic.
Yeah, I think this comment would be more accurate if it were inverted.
Original: "Lobsters is a polite garden party for very loud AI salesmen. Objecting to the garden party having been taken over by loud AI salesmen is impolite."
Inverted: "Lobsters is a polite garden party for very loud AI haters. Objecting to the garden party having been taken over by loud AI haters is impolite."
If aliens landed and had to pick one of these two to accurately describe comments on lobste.rs, they would pick the second one in an instant.
You and I have been members of this site for about a decade now.
I think you'd agree with me that the general vibe or tone of this community has been pro-FLOSS, anti-big tech for most of that time. Of course, there's always been people who have viewed this site as primarily a link aggregator with a focus on programming.
Now suddenly, the community has found that a significant subset of lobste.rs is fine with huge companies, or startups mostly funded by huge companies, scraping open source software and reselling access to it for a fee. Not only is this the future of software development, questioning this future is rude and uncouth.
I would say that @David_Gerard's analogy is the correct one.
This is very different.
The 10 most recent AI-related stories, starting with the front page:
Zero of these 10 have anything to do with "pro-FLOSS, anti-big tech" either in the post or the comments. Only one (the tenth, with 4 total upvotes) has comments that skew positive about AI.
If you look at all the comments across all of those posts, they are overwhelmingly negative on LLMs as a technology, regardless of FLOSSness.
If you look at all the comments across all of those posts, they are overwhelmingly negative on LLMs as a technology, regardless of FLOSSness.
Yes, the community tends to be negative about LLMs, for reasons including but not limited to they're something big tech is doing to us and they're stealing from FLOSS (and, speaking personally, everybody else releasing creative works to the public). You're not necessarily going to see the reasoning explicated in every single negative comment.
In many ways I think the rise of AI and the reaction against it on the site mirror cryptocurrency. Both are being pushed at a loss by people with deep pockets for reasons that have nothing to do with technology, both have practical negative externalities that their exponents don't seem interested in addressing, and both consist of some potentially useful technology wrapped up in an obviously and intrinsically malicious political purpose.
What is different is that a non-trivial number of people are fine with it this time, which is upsetting in itself tbh, and that the rest of us are explicitly restricted in what we can say about it. It feels like lobsters is AI-bro-friendly by fiat despite a broad consensus against.
Yes, the community tends to be negative about LLMs
It feels like lobsters is AI-bro-friendly by fiat despite a broad consensus against.
It sounds like we agree:
Well... yes. They are not a majority, but they're here, they're loud and they're protected despite being here against the will of the community, while opposition is policed. Or, to put it another way, they've taken over the party and we're being told objecting to that is impolite.
I can see why an AI salesman would feel unhappy about this, but it seems pretty obviously true that the problem with this party is people started selling AI in it.
Or, to put it another way, they've taken over the party
I don't know what "taken over the party" means when the vast majority of vibecoding-tagged posts on this community are anti-LLM. Is people who like LLMs simply talking too loud for you?
it seems pretty obviously true that the problem with this party is people started selling AI in it.
What about the longstanding community members who are bothered by AI hype, but bothered even more by the extreme level of anti-AI vitriol on nearly every discussion here where the topic comes up?
I mean it sounds like getting rid of the AI hype would solve both of their problems.
Let me put it this way: this is a community where for the most part things like externalities and engineering ethics are considered important. There are many reasons why AI might bother someone who cares about that; I mentioned one above, and I've talked about others before, but I'm not going to list them all.
To me, and apparently quite a lot of other people here, it is not much of a party when one is expected to smile and nod as someone enthuses about their latest collaboration with the worst thing to come out of the tech industry that's not attached to a reaction engine. I could hide from them and talk to other people and pretend like everything was fine, but I'd still be helping them sell AI just by being here.
I certainly don't think this is the ideal situation, and I'm sorry that the anti-AI comments bother you, but I'm glad AI stuff is so poorly received on the site. As soon as it is otherwise, it'll be unconscionable to stay.
"I agree fundamentally with what you are saying but disagree vehemently with the way you're saying it" is just tone policing.
to me it used to seem that lobsters' main advantage over hacker news was a focus on intellectual curiosity rather than industry gossip and business/finance. now there's a prominent topic of obvious relevance which people want to shut down for essentially anti-business reasons. this is more congenial than hn, but not what i expected. perhaps part of the community was more interested in what isn't here than in what is.
I don't understand what you mean here. Are you saying OP is a very loud AI salesperson? They've been vocally anti-LLM in the past, and this post is a challenge to AI people, so I don't think that's true.
Either way, I find it hard to believe lobste.rs has been taken over by "very loud AI salespeople" when the most popular posts with the vibecoding tag are anti-LLM, and anti-LLM comments frequently get 100+ upvotes in a site where triple digit scores are very rare.

None of the changes have any basis in psychology, they're all just 'some failed artist somewhere in Apple thought they looked cool'.

The first time you use their terminal emulator, Terminology, it presents a slider up front and center and asks you to adjust it until the text is a readable size.

The internals were terrifying. There’s a Daily WTF about it and it barely scratches the surface.
I remember attending a talk at FOSDEM by one of the Enlightenment folks who was proud of the layer they’d built to do FFI. I really struggled to explain to him that void*
and char*
is actually not sufficient type information on an argument to be able to do useful bridging from higher-level languages. I need to know the difference between a string and a data buffer, between an input and output parameter, between a pointer where the callee takes ownership or doesn’t, and so on.
At least we can be vaguely confident that the Apple window manager’s approach to passing data around isn’t just “Pass a void*
, God will know his own”. Never quite recovered from trying to read that code.

We don’t yet see major websites entirely built with webassembly-based frameworks. We’re not building our applications directly to WebAssembly for maximum portability. But why not?
Because WebAssembly can't do DOM things directly.
That means in practice your two options for building a Web UI are:
Wasm also can't directly create or manipulate the in-memory JS objects that all DOM APIs require, and converting between the two causes unavoidable performance overhead to using Wasm compared to JS.
Putting these together, using Wasm to build UIs in the browser requires starting from a position of both worse complexity and worse performance, and then it's up to the particular Wasm offering to deliver enough upsides to compensate for both of those unfixable downsides. That's a high bar to clear.
Actual benchmarks do not really corroborate DOM manipulation being a real performance problem for WebAssembly. If you care about code size a lot, that can be an issue though.
Whether it's more complex than just using JavaScript really depends on what you're doing and the rest of your stack.
On Twitter, I recall seeing some of the hostility (from people ostensibly using Rust) towards Fil-C, and I was a bit taken aback by it.
On one hand, it makes good sense to have an alternative for code which cannot be rewritten. But I suppose some level of tribalism/defensiveness over tech choices is to be expected as a community gets large over time... OTOH, the backlash against Rust programmers has also been noticeable and perhaps disproportionate...
Glad to see Graydon's writing, which can hopefully at least sway some of the people involved...
I think you can chum that up to negativity doing better on socials than positivity. Hot takes create engagement.
But that said, I've noticed that there's basically three camps when it comes to talking about memory safety.
Each one of these camps is right, and each is wrong, which makes it easy for all of them to argue with each other. And social media rewards toxicity even when it's unwarranted.
2 and 3 are both fine positions to have, but 1 is not.
It shouldn't be controversial to say that there exist programs where static and dynamic verification of memory safety is impossible, insufficient, or unnecessary. I include that in camp 1.
What's interesting about this discussion in the context of Rust is that it basically satisfies all the camps. You can write unsafe code that rustc can't verify, you can use runtime checks for safety, and you can use the compiler to statically verify swathes of code as correct.
I agree with that, but I don't agree that it is a "skill issue". Rather, it is a tooling deficiency.
I would say that it makes development several-fold slower, harder to maintain, and probably need more skilled people (thus also more expensive).
But I would only consider runtimes and such in this category, and absolutely agree that plain "skill issue" is just a wrong opinion to hold here.
(3) Static analysis is indeed a big aid. In aerospace, most companies use expensive tooling like Astrée (built on Galois connections), capable of guaranteeing the absence of a large class of bugs. The problem with the current open source funding model is that it is suboptimal for coordinating these kinds of efforts. Ideally, something like Astrée would be part of the e.g. GCC project. Instead, it's extremely expensive tooling that only big players can afford.
Frama-C tries to use an open source model, and it includes the same underlying techniques used by Astrée (abstract interpretation). It does not have the same level of polish as a full-fledged commercial product, but it can do sophisticated reasoning, and has a share of loyal industrial users (besides academic users).
One interesting aspect of its open source model is that some industrial users actually prefer it to be open source: they have been bitten in the past by proprietary applied research tools that became startups, thus making them pay twice: once to fund the applied research, then to buy the product they had indirectly helped develop. With the open source license, they are free to take the source code with them and pay someone else to maintain it.
From what I've seen, most people actually believe that two of your listed items are right and the third is wrong.
If I still had to worry about “professionalism”, then I'd pretend I'm in the middle camp (1 is wrong). But, my actual personal feeling is that 2 is wrong. And 3 is better than 1, not because it's safer, but because it's a flex to be able to prove things.
Oh god, we've just recreated Enneagram: "I'm a 2 programmer with a wing 3." 😆
(camp 2) Crashes are preferable to RCE and it's worth adding these guardrails for most programs, despite overhead.
Each one of these camps is right, and each is wrong
I disagree, because I don't see anyone in camp 3 arguing that crashes are not preferable to RCE (which, as I noted here, has actually murdered people -- not just killed, but enabled murder )
That is, or should be, a universal belief
i.e. nobody is going to say "well we can't find this stack overflow or use-after-free statically, so we should just make it undefined behavior and allow RCE, rather than crashing"
Rust also incurs runtime overhead to catch safety bugs, like array out of bounds, etc.
(Camp 1 is easily disproven any medium- or large-size development team. YOLO C can still be OK for one person hacking, but the nice thing is that Fil-C may be an immediate upgrade path which doesn't require a rewrite. Once your program reaches a certain size or threshold of usage, language- / platform-level memory safety is required)
That hostility is actively stoked by the Fil-C author trying to pitch his project as "safer than Rust" (because it's checking all pointer access, but at the same time has no data race safety at all) and downplaying the serious performance regressions it causes.
Fil-C is objectively safer than unsafe Rust. It's debatable if it's safer than safe Rust because it lacks expressiveness (See this discussion on X).
and downplaying the serious performance regressions it causes.
I don't think the performance regressions are necessarily a problem in all cases, or as much of a deal as people are claiming. There is also quite a bit of potential in making it faster with the current guarantees that it has.
Interesting thread, and linked gist, on the limitations of Fil-C regarding pointer/capability tearing. (https://gist.github.com/unixpickle/4eaae977d79c3b9eeda45d5baf52859f)
I tried the test program. My first run yielded 'secret password', the second run caused a trap.
Interesting thread on X. Only half-related to this discussion, but I'm on the opinion that if we generally agree to call Go memory safe even though tearing can literally cause segfaults there, it is more than fair game to call Fil-C also memory safe, in colloquial terms.
Go memory safe even though tearing can literally cause segfaults there
Seg faults are the safe behavior in that case. The unsafe behavior is reading the wrong value due to tearing. and allowing execution to continue.
e.g. see this thread and the 5 years of other threads linked - https://lobste.rs/s/xyg1fu/fil_c#c_j6smgk
The approach outlined by Fil-C gives memory safety -- full stop -- not just in colloquial terms
In other words, if you have a learned/acquired an informal definition of the word "safe" from Rust, then you should replace that in your head with a definition that was established before Rust existed. This definition is more precise, and more useful in practice.
On the tearing issue, I believe there are instructions available (x86, x86_64, possibly ARM64) which would allow a 2x pointer-width value to be written in an atomic operation. Which would avoid the tearing when updating an interface variable. I'm surprised the code gen doesn't use them.
Which would then still leave the well documented non concurrency safe access to maps, or is there something else I may have forgotten?
I don't think we call Go memory safe? It's a far sight better in practice than C, but memory safety is a binary property that go doesn't have. I haven't entirely made my mind up about Fil-C yet (largely because I don't understand what exactly it does/doesn't guarantee).
Golang is frequently described as memory safe, sometimes with the data race caveat
https://www.memorysafety.org/docs/memory-safety/
https://research.google/pubs/secure-by-design-googles-perspective-on-memory-safety/
https://media.defense.gov/2023/Apr/27/2003210083/-1/-1/0/CSI_SOFTWARE_MEMORY_SAFETY_V1.1.PDF
https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/2023-12/The-Case-for-Memory-Safe-Roadmaps-508c.pdf
It is absolutely called a memory safe language by most people, and as many CS terms, it doesn't have a single, agreed upon definition. Like, strictly speaking basically no managed language that has FFI-support can ever be safe. But that would make the definition pretty useless.
I think most people call Go memory-safe, although I agree it's reasonable to question that.
To be fair, the line we collectively draw around what languages qualify as "memory-safe" is somewhat arbitrary, considering every mainstream programming language is designed to let the programmer create memory-unsafe programs - with the only exception being JavaScript running in a browser:
I think in practice "memory safe" is not really a binary property. Python has long unresolved issues with buffer garbage collection which makes code like this crash:
import io
class BadFile(io.RawIOBase):
def readinto(self, b):
global buf
buf = b.cast('P')
def readable(self):
return True
io.BufferedReader(BadFile()).read(1)
l = [None] * len(buf)
buf[0] = 0
print(l[0])
Does that make Python a non memory safe language?
I don't think implementation bugs matter, rather the intended behavior of the language.
So if Go where to say that tearing is not intended, just also not highest priority it would be memory safe? Because this Python issue is as low priority there for 10 years or more and it's fundamental limitation of buffers on the C side.
I'd say the distinction is "Python's implementation currently has a bug which results in memory unsafety, and that bug could be fixed at any time without changing Python's design" versus (as I understand it) "Go's design leads to memory unsafety in all correct Go implementations, and the only way to remove the memory unsafety would be to change the language's design."
That distinction is relevant because all language implementations can have bugs with a wide range of symptoms, so it's reasonable to put "this particular bug exists in this particular implementation" in a different category from design issues.
I'd say the distinction is "Python's implementation currently has a bug which results in memory unsafety, and that bug could be fixed at any time without changing Python's design"
That's a bit tricky because this "bug" cannot be fixed without changing CPython's entire buffer system which would break all native extensions out there. While yes, sure, on the Python language front it might not be intentional, the CABI is stable and also part of the language.
Ah, I wasn't aware of that distinction! (I don't use Python or Go; I was basing that on other comments in the thread.)
Yeah, the flip side would be that when I encountered this in the wild (and I have, in production code written by a pretty good coworker, in not even that much go programming) I would be criticizing the go compiler for being buggy...
I definitely think go made the right decision here by by documenting how it actually works at the cost of the language not being memory safe in the presence of data races.
I've programmed a fair bit more python in my life than go, and I've never encountered a memory safety bug in it, by comparison.
Why doesn't Go have that property? (honest question, I don't use Go)
Here is a related thread, which is full of the same misconception that a seg fault on data race means Go is not memory safe.
GoLang is also memory-safe? - https://old.reddit.com/r/rust/comments/1gbksec/golang_is_also_memorysafe/
As mentioned below, a seg fault is the safe behavior in that case. The unsafe behavior is executing code after tearing has altered the meaning of the program.
This is just like a Rust program crashing on array index-out-of-bounds is the safe behavior. The unsafe behavior is not crashing and reading invalid memory, like C.
As mentioned below, a seg fault is the safe behavior in that case. The unsafe behavior is executing code after tearing has altered the meaning of the program.
This is just like a Rust program crashing on array index-out-of-bounds is the safe behavior. The unsafe behavior is not crashing and reading invalid memory, like C.
This is btw. the reason why Rust moved away from the "avoids segfaults" messaging that was on the very first website. "We panic instead" (which is a language-level expression of this behaviour) is just moving a reasonable approach to a different place.
A segfault is the "happy path" of the unhappy path. This is not about guaranteed segfaults (like reading an actually nulled nullptr) - which you could actually just handle yourself.
Rust's panic is a specified behavior, unlike this.
Why doesn't Go have it? (honest question, I don't use Go)
Go makes data-races atomic at a per field level, and then has various built in types like slices and maps that store (pointer, length)/(pointer, type)/... pairs and considers those different fields but trusts that they are consistent. So if you try hard enough you can read/write to arbitrary memory by racing on writing to those pairs and reading from those pairs. This is documented in the memory model.
you can read/write to arbitrary memory by racing on writing to those pairs
How? The doc you linked appears to define the behavior:
An implementation may always react to a data race by reporting the race and terminating the program.
Otherwise, each read of a single-word-sized or sub-word-sized memory location must observe a value actually written to that location (perhaps by a concurrent executing goroutine) and not yet overwritten.
These implementation constraints make Go more like Java or JavaScript, in that most races have a limited number of outcomes, and less like C and C++, where the meaning of any program with a race is entirely undefined, and the compiler may do anything at all.
i.e. I am reading that bad reads due to tearing cannot occur -- you either read a value you actually wrote, or the program crashes
So it's not a single behavior, but it is defined.
Because you start with a slice {ptr: ptr1, len: 10}
and a second slice {ptr: ptr2, len: 100000}
and then through a race you manufacture a slice {ptr: ptr1, len: 100000}
and you just do normal slice operations on that new incorrect slice which think its allowed to access memory more than 10 bytes after ptr1
.
The spec makes this pretty explicit
When the values depend on the consistency of internal (pointer, length) or (pointer, type) pairs, as can be the case for interface values, maps, slices, and strings in most Go implementations, such races can in turn lead to arbitrary memory corruption.
OK thanks! So, using the definition of Cardelli [1], Go is not a safe language, because the program you showed has untrapped errors (i.e. errors that do not cause a crash)
(Notably, the slice issue is different than what a comment above said -- which is that data races may cause a crash. That doesn't make Go unsafe)
On the other hand, I would call Fil-C safe. It probably has some holes now, but it's more a matter of intention than having zero bugs.
That is, if a program has undefined behavior that is not trapped -- is the fault of the compiler and/or runtime, or is that the fault of the author of that program?
Fil-C is saying it's the fault of the compiler/runtime, so it is a safe language. They are explicitly choosing performance overhead over undefined behavior.
[1] It is useful to distinguish between two kinds of execution errors: the ones that cause the computation to stop immediately, and the ones that go unnoticed (for a while) and later cause arbitrary behavior. The former are called trapped errors, whereas the latter are untrapped errors.
A program fragment is safe if it does not cause untrapped errors to occur. Languages where all program fragments are safe are called safe languages. Therefore, safe languages rule out the most insidious form of execution errors: the ones that may go unnoticed.

I had this conversation with the author. His position was that data races are UB in C and anything that atomically updates a pointer must use the LLVM IR atomics. If you store to a pointer in one thread and load in another, you may have tearing between the address and capability parts of a pointer.
The problem with this is that the last mainstream (including embedded) processor that did not do atomic loads and stores for pointers was the 386SX. This means that a lot C code (especially C code that predates the C11 memory model, i.e. most of it) assumes that if you store to a pointer-typed global in one thread and read it in another, you will get behaviour equivalent to relaxed-consistency atomics: the loading thread will see either the old or new value (it's undefined which, but that's fine). In some places, people have migrated to slapping _Atomic
on these and using the stdatomic.h
helpers to do relaxed-consistency operations, but it's never been a priority, for two reasons:
This is particularly common in low-level TCB code, where availability matters. With Fil-C, this will crash randomly in a way that is hard to debug.
Would code that doesn't use _Atomic
for these be buggy when compiled for ARM?
Nope. Bare loads and stores on Arm are relaxed-consistency atomic. If you do a store-word from one core on Arm and a load-word on another core, the load will see either the old value or the new one.
Atomic is a bit overloaded, when people talk about atomics they actually mean two things: atomicity (meaning that the loads and stores are indivisible) and ordering. Without _Atomic
, single-word stores are atomic but not ordered: it is entirely undefined (in the absence of other barriers elsewhere) whether you will see the old version or the new version. If you do two stores, one to location A and then another to B, another core may see result of the store to B but the old version and the new version of A.
Some processors had a narrower memory bus than the pointer width. For example, the 8088 had an 8-bit memory bus and 16-bit pointers (well, slightly more complicated than that). The 386SX had a 16-bit memory bus with 32-bit pointers. This meant that a store of a pointer was split into two stores, one cycle apart. If you had multiple bus masters (including multiple processors) then there could be some interleaving. If you built an SMP system with 386SX processors (I know some people build SMP 386s, but I think they used DXs, which had a 32-bit memory bus), then one core could start doing a store, store half of a word, then another one could do a load and see the new low 16 bits and the high old 16 bits.
But no vaguely modern or widespread multiprocessor systems have looked like that. Pretty much very real SMP system has had the property that storing a register value with no explicit atomic operations is atomic but unordered. It is a single store to a local cache line in exclusive state. It may be in store queues and take a while to reach the cache, but any other core either sees an old version of the cache line or a new one.
Because the state for Fil-C pointers is split between the address and capability (metadata) parts, and these are in separate cache lines, it loses that property, a property that has been true of any real C implementation since the beginning (single-core systems that run C have this property even with narrower address busses, because an interrupt will happen after the pair of stores, it's only with respect to DMA that they might be interleaved).
Note that everything you're talking about for pointer-sized words is guaranteed by the hardware platform but not the compiler. The compiler is allowed to amplify races on non-_Atomic
s if enough inlining happens.
Again, that relates to ordering, not atomicity. The compiler may, for example, turn a loop that is guarded on a variable being updated in another thread into an infinite loop if the load of the variable is not atomic (or volatile). That’s fine because it is simply moving all loads of the stored value indefinitely far into the future. But it will not introduce tearing.

As was mentioned elsewhere on this page, the C standard does not guarantee that any variable (pointers or otherwise) is atomically updated unless it is marked as being _Atomic. So only guaranteed for things so marked after C11.

On porting code from one language to another -- There's an interesting tradeoff between reuse and duplication.

There was a programming movement some 25 years ago focused around the principle “don’t repeat yourself.” As is so often the case with short snappy principles taught to undergrads, it got taken too far.
....
The past 10-15 years has seen a far more tempered approach to writing code, with many programmers understanding it is better to reimplement a concept if the cost of sharing the implementation is higher than the cost of implementing and maintaining separate code.
In other words, LLMs make bespoke code cheap. I think this is right, but it will ALSO be taken too far!
As with most things, I view it as a combinatorial / scaling problem. The cost of ~100K or ~1M lines of LLM-translated code in an application is not zero ... in terms of the humans who have to work with it
A future where say the Go, Zig, Rust, Swift, Roc ecosystems are constantly LLM-porting each other's code back and forth seems a little silly. We're going to take it too far, and probably swing back back to plain-old API design / language design / library reuse, which are hard things ...
On the larger question about new programming languages ...
One way to view programming language design is as a form of compression ... [1] You're trying to express some class of programs in a syntax and semantics that is easy for both a human and computer to understand
And I think that is going to survive, for fundamental reasons. LLMs don't change the "human" part -- if anything, they amplify its importance!
A thought experiment: Do you expect LLMs to generate raw assembly code, making programming languages obsolete?
I claim that if you do, then you have misunderstood what LLMs are ... they are language wizards, and they LIKE using different programming languages.
So my intuition is that these three things are basically the same:
So if you believe that, then programming language design is still valuable, and there will be future progress in the field. (I also suspect that LLMs will "like" to develop their OWN DSLs / languages to solve problems, for the same fundamental reason of compression)
I'm not sure if anyone has written about this, but if so, I'd like to see it ! (or an argument against it)
[1] Similar to LLMs themselves being a form of compression: https://bellard.org/ts_zip/
FWIW, https://oils.pub/ is a bit hedged -- LLMs are extremely good at writing OSH, because it's the most bash-compatible shell - https://pages.oils.pub/spec-compat/2025-06-26/renamed-tmp/spec/compat/TOP.html
On the other hand, you can say that "YSH is for humans" ... it has more power than bash, but it's smaller and easier to remember (or will be, once we finish disallowing the bash features that have been generalized / replaced)
I just got word that one of our contributors replaced a ~2500 line bash script with a ~2000 line YSH script at work, with HUMAN coworkers who didn't know YSH beforehand. So that is a good sign!

My experience prior to Claude 4 was that AI agents in particular would be totally fine at writing syntactically valid Rust, but they would get stuck on borrow checker errors on a regular basis (as in, get into a state where they give up and deleted everything they'd attempted to write and replaced it with "// A real implementation would ..."), but since Claude 4 that failure mode has pretty much disappeared in my experience.

The difference lies in the nature of LLMs as tools. They're nondeterministic, fuzzy, with a near infinity of applications that other tools can't come close to touching in breadth. What interesting techniques can I read about to squeeze out performance here?
I'm guessing this is already on your radar since you mentioned data-oriented design, but I think the single most important piece of information you could consume on how to write a fast compiler is Andrew Kelley's 2021 talk at Handmade Seattle, "Practical Data Oriented Design":
https://youtu.be/IroPQ150F6c?si=o6g4Xh7aI_RiAiYK
Given your goals, I'd advise you to be careful what advice you trust when it comes to compiler performance. A lot of "best practice" advice around compiler performance will get you status quo performance outcomes, and you're aiming much higher than that. In particular, I'd trust sources that have actually implemented fast compilers (such as Andrew) and be very skeptical of advice based on compilers whose performance you find unacceptable.
That said, you might not have a choice in some cases; there might not be any sufficiently fast compilers that have the type system features you want. For example, among ML-family languages, the fastest compilers I'm aware of are OCaml, Elm, and Roc, and none of them support higher-kinded polymorphism. If you want to know how to implement HKP with maximum performance, I'm guessing GHC is probably your best bet.
In general, the best advice I've found on these things is talking to compiler authors who have had people using their compilers on large code bases in anger. Of course, nothing beats getting actual performance numbers out of your compiler; there will always be surprises there! Literature tends to focus heavily on asymptotic complexity - which is a very important factor - but while hand-waving the in-practice overhead required to achieve that asymptotic complexity. A very notable exception is Daniel Lemire's work.

What are the non-obvious things, that seem cheap but are expensive or seem expensive but are cheap?
Something I found non-obvious is that high-quality error messages have a performance cost. As a super basic example, if you want to be able to report the source code locations of your type mismatches, then you have to be tracking that information all the way through your type-checking data structures instead of not bothering to propagate it from earlier phases.
Basically every compiler does this, but I use it as an example because there are less-obvious cases where giving a better error message similarly requires tracking more information, which in turn creates more work for the compiler.
I'm in favor of high-quality error messages, but it's worth noting that they are in tension with maximum compiler performance.
What combos interact poorly, performance-wise?
I don't have firsthand experience with it, but I have heard that subtyping and type inference interact poorly. It sounds like you're going for type unification (like Haskell, Elm, Roc, etc. do) rather than subtyping, which doesn't have that problem, but I thought I'd mention it.
Faster runtime performance makes it harder to get faster compile times, but since you're not trying to go super fast at runtime too, you'll have an easier time getting fast compiler times than we have with Roc. :)
Finally, linking; I had the thought of doing my own linking and then discovered that Roc does exactly that, which is an encouraging sign. I don’t think there’s anything much to say here. Linking is probably the slowest part for most compilers, it seems. It’s extremely frustrating.
If you don't care about runtime performance, then I wouldn't do any linking at all - just have an interpreter and that's it!
Of note, in Roc we do our own linking on Linux and Windows. We have attempted to get the same thing working on macOS and have not succeeded. Mach-O is wildly different than ELF or PE/COFF, and not only is it not (accurately) documented, we've talked to people who have talked to people at Apple who basically say that nobody who still works there really knows how it works either. I'm guessing the way lld got macOS linking working was reversing the system linker, which might be why lld was broken on macOS for years...but in our case we didn't need to just do a subset of what a system linker was doing, we needed to do a specific different thing.
Anyway, what we're planning to try next is to have our new development backend pre-link (using lld, which we'll embed in the compiler binary) a Roc interpreter into whatever Roc Platform you're building on, and then that way when you're running a debug build it won't have to link at all. If the interpreter turns out to be too slow in practice, we can change it to dynamically link a compiled Roc program as a dylib on startup, which is not ideal either, but should be much less linking work than a full static link.
For optimized builds, we'll use lld. This will make optimized builds take longer to link, but since those are done much less frequently (and will already be unavoidably slow because we use LLVM for optimized builds, which takes a long time to do its job), that seems like the right tradeoff to make.

Haskell was not inspired as directly or as immediately by Backus’s Turing Award lecture as you suggest. There was a whole decade between them! And Backus was talking about functional programming in general, not specifically about lazy evaluation.
The Haskell Report says it was inspired by
an unfortunate situation in the functional programming community: there had come into being more than a dozen non-strict, purely functional programming languages, all similar in expressive power and semantic underpinnings.
There’s much more in the HOPL paper; Backus features, of course, but he isn’t a major influence.
https://simon.peytonjones.org/history-of-haskell/
The successor to lazy evaluation is total functional programming, in which evaluation is strongly normalizing, and therefore order of evaluation is moot. David Turner (a pioneer of lazy evaluation) wrote a paper about it, and before that it’s long been a foundational part of dependent type systems and proof assistants.
My understanding of that time period comes from SPJ here:
https://youtu.be/06x8Wf2r2Mc?si=iwyjvT7npWfXxivO&t=153
At least the way SPJ tells it, that "unfortunate situation in the functional programming community" was a direct result of their being inspired by Backus:
Into this highly volatile mixture of ideas that intoxicated us, God arrived in the form of John Backus. And he gave the Turing Award lecture under the title "Can Programming be Liberated from the Von Neumann Style?" [...] This was like lighting a fire [on] this firewood that had been building up, because it made us think that functional programming was respectable, and God had said: "Go forth, and build new languages, and better still, new computers to execute them!" There is nothing that young researchers like better than a call to arms like that. So off we went, and it's hard to explain exactly what it felt like. I think it's kind of like being on drugs...and boy, did we inhale! And the result, as you might expect, was complete chaos.
In that quote, SPJ calls Backus "God" twice, suggests that prior to reading him they hadn't considered FP a "respectable" pursuit, and says that Backus's call-to-arms to create new languages was what inspired the researchers to race off and work on those new languages - which led to the "complete chaos" of "more than a dozen non-strict, purely functional programming languages" that in turn motivated the Haskell Committee.
Of course, it's possible that this is just SPJ being an engaging storyteller and not exactly how it happened. :)
It’s a mistake to skip over the three minutes that Peyton Jones spent listing other inspiring work that happened before Backus’s endorsement, and imply that Backus was the only inspiration. The “chaos” slide is also important because that covers a whole decade of research into functional programming and non-Von Neumann architectures, but the way you wrote “specifically” suggests that the Haskell committee was formed in 1978 not 1987. Haskell was started because of the chaos that followed the excitement (not because of the excitement itself), and during that excitement and chaos the potential of laziness had already been demonstrated.

Sure!
So in Roc, we have the platform/application split - https://www.roc-lang.org/platforms
Let's say I'm building a CLI application on the basic-cli platform - https://github.com/roc-lang/basic-cli
If I do roc build --release
, I should end up with a single standalone binary executable CLI that doesn't need to dynamically link my program. (It might dynamically link libc or something like that, but only if the author of the basic-cli platform decided to. roc build --release
must never add any dynamically linked libraries to the platform beyond what was already there.)
The plan for how we will accomplish this is:
roc build
will generate a *.o binary file that is not actually an executable, but rather which just provides some symbols (maybe just one - e.g. an entrypoint named roc_main
or something) that the platform will later link with.All of this sounds like a lot of trouble, but it means that:
roc build --release
and it will Just Work (unless the platform author included dylibs which aren't on your system, which we can't prevent in the general case because some use cases just unavoidably require dylib use). Since platform authors are only allowed to statically link files which they themselves have distributed in the tarball, and they have done this for all targets which they support, and linking is always done using the lld bundled with the Roc compiler, we can know in advance that linking is going to succeed regardless of what static libs you have on your system and in what locations.roc
on a Mac, you have all the object files necessary to build for Windows, and lld knows how to link for all the different targets (again, regardless of which machine it's being run on). Certainly LLVM, which we also use in --release
, knows how to build for all the targets too.roc build --release
to get a static binary that works on your machine, you can also do roc build --release --target=blah
to build it for another machine, and that will also Just Work (as long as the platform supports that target, of course).Okay, so that's release builds. The reason we don't want to do that process in development builds is:
So we don't want to do either of those in a development build.
Instead, what we plan to do for development builds is to do everything I just said once, right when you download the platform, to build a version of your application where all your entrypoints (e.g. roc_main
mentioned above) have been replaced with implementations of a Roc interpreter which loads your .roc source files, interprets them, and sends the result to the platform. Importantly, this can be built once and then cached; you don't need to rebuild it whenever your code changes - instead, you can just re-run the prebuilt binary and it'll start interpreting as soon as the Roc code gets invoked.
If the interpreter ends up being too slow at runtime, we can do the same strategy except we build a dylib (skipping LLVM and going straight to machine code like we currently do) and instead of having the pre-linked binary start an interpreter inside the Roc entrypoints, it instead does a dlopen to load the dylib that roc build
built.
Anyway, that's the plan! We'll see how it goes in practice. :)

In summary, the series didn't present me with any compelling upsides to lazy evaluation, but it did present several major downsides. So although there was a lot of really well-done technical explanation in the series, it definitely reinforced my perception that laziness is a dead-end. 😄
